text: Despite a wealth of literature regarding public participation in spatial planning in the recent decades, many instances of spatial planning can still be seen to create a divide between policy makers and inhabitants. Therefore, participation in spatial planning is one of the main objects of study in various fields of urban planning science. The most interest has been directed towards the instances of participation as a reaction to implemented planning decisions or measures, whereas very little is known about initiatives taken towards the development of empty or declining urban spaces. Such developments often begin with new uses being introduced in these spaces, and frequently are implemented via physical alterations. The purpose of this paper is to outline various different types of local developments and focus, above all, on the difference between reactive and constructive types of initiatives and their relation to community concepts. The paper is based on research into two instances of developments of neglected urban spaces. Between these instances exist considerable differences, which have been utilised as basis for drawing the conclusions of this study, highlighting possibilities for developing constructive types of participation. Moreover, based on these experiences, the study presents proposals on increasing the effectiveness of the present planning system and, therefore, opening opportunities for the development of unused space especially in times of economic decline when such spaces are growing rapidly. This is due to the complex nature and inefficiency of present planning system, which deters too many inspiring developments.
